summary: A manager's disastrous attempt to use AI to generate 180 lines of dbt code illustrates why AI won't replace data engineers anytime soon. The post argues that 'vibe coding' (letting AI do the work while you push buttons) leads to professional negligence, while 'guide coding' (AI as an assistant) can be beneficial if you understand fundamentals.
key_points: AI-generated code disasters show we're far from AI replacing data engineers
There's a crucial difference between 'guide coding' (AI assists) and 'vibe coding' (AI does everything)
Deploying AI-generated code you don't understand is professional malpractice
Speed from AI is useless if it causes more harm than good
Foundational knowledge and understanding your craft are prerequisites for beneficial AI use
money_quotes: There's no 'I' in Team, but there's an 'I' in vibe.
The question always comes back to whether you understand what it is you (or AI) built, and whether it adequately addresses the problem you're solving.
If you're like this manager and team, who doesn't understand what was built, especially irresponsibly deploying this to production like a maniac, then this is professional negligence and malpractice.
The definition of stupid behavior is hurting yourself while you hurt others.
Speed on its own is useless if it causes you more harm than good.
key_insight: AI is a powerful tool that amplifies both competence and incompetence—without foundational knowledge and understanding of your craft, you're just creating faster disasters.
